verdict_reasoning: BAX is canonically a cytosolic/cytoplasmic pro-apoptotic BCL-2 family protein. Under baseline conditions it resides in the cytosol as a soluble monomer or loosely associates with the outer mitochondrial membrane. However, upon apoptotic activation, BAX undergoes conformational change, inserts into the outer mitochondrial membrane, and critically also translocates to and inserts into the plasma membrane outer leaflet on dying/apoptotic cells. This cell-death-induced PM exposure is well-documented: antibodies recognizing the N-terminal or loop domains of BAX detect surface-exposed BAX on apoptotic cells by flow cytometry and surface biotinylation. This qualifies as contextual/cell_state_induced (apoptosis/ICD-linked PM translocation). No stable baseline surface pool exists under normal non-apoptotic conditions, so 'yes' is not warranted. The five contextual buckets: cell_state_induced — YES (apoptosis/ICD); tissue_restricted_surface — no evidence; lysosomal_exocytosis — no; dual_localization — no stable PM cycling pool; stable_surface_attachment — no TM-partner tethering. Cancer-cell ecto-BAX has also been reported.
